phrasal_verbs.meaning: analyse something by separating it into smaller parts
use something as a foundation in order to develop further
begin to understand or realise something
gradually change your opinion or decision about something
investigate or study something thoroughly and in depth
make use of knowledge, experience or resources
think or talk about something for a long time, especially something unpleasant
use something when other options have failed or are unavailable
succeed in communicating an idea or message to someone
maintain the same level or pace as someone or something
learn a skill or language informally, without being taught
continue doing something despite difficulties or opposition
begin a task or journey with a clear intention; explain or describe something clearly
calculate something or find the answer to a problem; understand someone or something
dismiss something or someone as unimportant or beyond improvement

impression: make a strong impression on sb
mind: in the right frame of mind
at the back of your mind
question: call into question
raise a question
open question
beyond question
pressing question
a moot question
put the question to sb
out of the question
without question
side: the other side of the coin
view: in the light of this view

idioms.meaning: not knowing what to do or say in a difficult situation
be unable to remember or find something, even after trying hard
make an effort to understand or deal with something difficult
be too difficult for someone to understand

idioms.idiom: have a lot on your plate
idioms.meaning: have a great deal to do or a lot of problems to deal with
make sure someone continues to receive all the relevant information
learn the basic skills needed for a particular job or activity
fail to take advantage of a good opportunity

idioms.idiom: on the tip of your tongue
idioms.meaning: almost able to remember a word or piece of information
ask someone a lot of questions in order to get information or ideas from them

questions.sentence: I couldn't fully **absorb / assess** all the information in such a short time.
It was the scientist who first **hypothesised / predicted** that the compound would have this effect.
The report was **scrutinised / evaluated** very carefully by three independent reviewers before publication.
Can you **articulate / assert** your concerns more clearly for the rest of the group?
His **coherent / deliberate** argument won over even the most cautious members of the audience.
She was extremely **attentive / cautious** throughout the lecture and took notes on every key point.
I can only **surmise / foresee** what actually happened that night, as I wasn't there.
Researchers were able to **identify / recognise** three distinct patterns in the language data.
The new findings **contradict / dispute** the results of all previous studies on this topic.
She chose to **elaborate / evaluate** on her initial findings during the follow-up presentation.

B.title: Complete using the correct form of the words
B.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box. More than one word might fit in each gap, but try to use all the words.

questions.sentence: It was clear that Dr Harris had thoroughly …………… the data before presenting her findings.

She …………… the criticism gracefully, promising to work on her areas of weakness.
Without direct evidence, scientists could only …………… about the source of the anomaly.
He …………… the delay as a minor inconvenience, even though it had set him back by two weeks.

D.title: Choose the correct word
D.instructions: Choose the correct word (A, B, C or D) to complete each gap in the text.
D.passage:
When students (1) ...... at university, many find the experience quite different from what they expected. Rather than following a fixed schedule, they are expected to be more (2) ...... learners, setting their own goals and managing their own time. Some universities now offer (3) ...... programmes, which combine online study with face-to-face classes. Students with a natural (4) ...... for a subject often thrive in this type of environment. However, (5) ...... students, who show exceptional ability across all subjects, may find that standard courses are insufficiently challenging.

For students who lack basic (6) ...... and (7) ...... skills, additional support is crucial. Without these foundational skills, it is difficult to (8) ...... new academic knowledge effectively. A (9) ...... programme of study may be necessary to address these gaps. At the end of their studies, students receive an official (10) ...... listing all the modules they have successfully completed.

questions.sentence: When students (1) ...... at university, many find the experience quite different from what they expected.
Rather than following a fixed schedule, they are expected to be more (2) ...... learners.
Some universities now offer (3) ...... programmes, which combine online study with face-to-face classes.
Students with a natural (4) ...... for a subject often thrive in this type of environment.
However, (5) ...... students, who show exceptional ability across all subjects, may find that standard courses are insufficiently challenging.
For students who lack basic (6) ...... skills, additional support is crucial.
For students who lack basic literacy and (7) ...... skills, additional support is crucial.
Without these foundational skills, it is difficult to (8) ...... new academic knowledge effectively.
A (9) ...... programme of study may be necessary to address these gaps.
At the end of their studies, students receive an official (10) ...... listing all the modules they have successfully completed.

E.title: Match to make sentences
E.instructions: Match the sentence beginnings (1–7) with the correct endings (A–G).

questions.sentence: He was determined to build **C** on this early research and develop his ideas further.
questions.answer: C – on this early research and develop his ideas further.

questions.sentence: The journalist spent months delving **F** into the claims made in the original paper.
questions.answer: F – into the claims made in the original paper.

questions.sentence: She always draws **A** on her extensive experience when advising new students.
questions.answer: A – on her extensive experience when advising new students.

questions.sentence: She found it very difficult to get her key findings **E** across to a non-specialist audience.
questions.answer: E – across to a non-specialist audience.

questions.sentence: Despite the setbacks, she pressed **G** on and eventually completed her doctorate.
questions.answer: G – on and eventually completed her doctorate.

questions.sentence: He picked **B** up conversational Spanish very quickly while working abroad.
questions.answer: B – up conversational Spanish very quickly while working abroad.

questions.sentence: It took me a while to work **D** out how to navigate the university’s online library system.
questions.answer: D – out how to navigate the university’s online library system.

F.title: Write one word in each gap (phrasal verbs)
F.instructions: Write one word in each gap so that each second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

questions.sentence:
No one could understand how the experiment had succeeded.
No one could work …………… how the experiment had succeeded.

She uses her years of experience when giving advice.
She draws …………… her years of experience when giving advice.

She gradually came to appreciate the value of structured debate.
She gradually came …………… to appreciating the value of structured debate.

The new theory is built upon earlier discoveries.
The new theory builds …………… earlier discoveries.

She continued working on her thesis despite all the opposition.
She pressed …………… with her thesis despite all the opposition.

He learned some Italian without a teacher, just from listening.
He just picked it …………… while living in Italy.

The committee investigated the proposed changes to the curriculum.
The committee looked carefully …………… the proposed changes to the curriculum.

He managed to communicate the idea effectively to his students.
He got the idea …………… to his students effectively.

She analysed the argument by dividing it into separate parts.
She broke the argument …………… into its separate parts.

G.title: Phrasal verbs with into
G.instructions: Phrasal verbs with into, such as delve into, are often connected to the idea of investigating or studying something. Which of these phrasal verbs with into are also connected to this idea?

questions.sentence: She decided to **look into** the matter more carefully before drawing any conclusions.
The train **pulled into** the station exactly on time.
Can you **dig into** the background of this study for me?
They **ran into** some unexpected difficulties with the experiment.
He likes to **probe into** the underlying assumptions of any theory.
The children **burst into** laughter when the teacher made a joke.
She always **inquires into** the sources of any claim she encounters.
We **bumped into** our old professor at the conference.
The inspector decided to **pry into** the company’s accounting practices.
She **walked into** the examination hall feeling calm and well-prepared.

H.title: Write one word in each gap (collocations)
H.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: The witness was asked to give a full …………… of the events of that evening.
I will always …………… this piece of music with long evenings studying in the university library.
You need to …………… a balance between coursework and revision if you want to do well.

She continued to study hard …………… the belief that her dedication would eventually pay off.
I’ve been racking my …………… trying to remember the name of the theorem.
After weeks of analysis, the research team finally …………… to a conclusion.
Students should always take cultural differences …………… consideration when writing about global issues.
These new findings …………… serious doubt on the reliability of the original study.
Completing her doctoral thesis was a …………… come true.
The seminar was mainly …………… on the social consequences of digital illiteracy.
I was …………… the misapprehension that the deadline had already been extended.
She made a quick …………… note to look up that reference before submitting her essay.
It’s important to keep an open …………… when reading conflicting academic opinions.
Studying abroad gave him a fresh …………… on his own cultural assumptions.

I.title: One word for three sentences
I.instructions: For each question, write one word which can be used in all three sentences.

questions.sentence:
She always gives you a …………… answer, no matter how uncomfortable the truth might be.
Come …………… to the point – what exactly are you proposing?
I couldn’t think …………… after staying up all night to write the essay.

From my point of ……………, the argument was unconvincing.
She took the firm …………… that all assessments should be open-book.
The report presented a balanced …………… of the topic from multiple angles.

Let’s look on the bright …………… – at least we learned from the mistake.
She always tries to see the other person’s …………… of things before forming an opinion.
On the positive ……………, the new approach has saved us a significant amount of time.

The entire validity of the study has been called into …………….
Whether this approach is sustainable is still an open …………….
Without ……………, this is the most comprehensive textbook available on the subject.

I couldn’t make …………… of the lecturer’s notes without the handout.
It’s just common …………… to save your work before closing the programme.

In ……………, I would like to thank all my supervisors for their invaluable support.

J.title: Write one word in each gap (text)
J.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: I read last month about a new movement in education called ‘slow learning’. The founder argues that it is (1) …………… her principles to rush students through content before they have had time to truly (2) …………… it in. She started the movement (3) …………… the belief that many students lack the depth of understanding needed for real academic (4) ……………. There is no (5) …………… of any reduction in the volume of the curriculum – if anything, it is growing – but the slow learning approach has been set up with a (6) …………… to prioritising depth over breadth.
questions.answer: 1 against, 2 absorb, 3 in, 4 competence, 5 question, 6 view

K.title: Key word transformation
K.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

questions.sentence:
Nobody expected the experiment to produce such striking results.
**anticipated** Nobody …………………………………………… such striking results from the experiment.

I don’t understand why she decided to change her research focus.
**fathom** I cannot …………………………………………… she decided to change her research focus.

She finally accepted the validity of the criticism.
**came** She finally …………………………………………… the validity of the criticism.

The university thoroughly investigated the allegations.
**looked** The university …………………………………………… the allegations thoroughly.

I personally believe that the argument is fundamentally flawed.
**view** In my …………………………………………… fundamentally flawed.

She didn’t inform Marcus because she didn’t want to upset his feelings.
**consideration** She didn’t inform Marcus …………………………………………… his feelings.

There is no question that critical thinking is the most important academic skill.
**doubt** There is …………………………………………… that critical thinking is the most important academic skill.

You must consider all possible outcomes before making a decision.
**account** You must …………………………………………… all possible outcomes before making a decision.

L.title: Complete idioms
L.instructions: Complete using the words in the box.

questions.sentence: I was completely at a …………… when the examiner asked me to justify my methodology.
I drew a complete …………… when asked to name the author of the original study.

Please keep me in the …………… with any new developments in the research.
As a new PhD student, it took him a while to learn the …………….
We nearly missed the …………… on that research grant – the deadline was yesterday!
Her name is right on the tip of my ……………. I just can’t recall it.

M.title: Word formation
M.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

questions.sentence: The committee produced a thorough …………… of the proposed changes to the academic calendar. **EVALUATE**
His …………… of the data differed significantly from his colleague’s. **INTERPRET**
Early scientists put forward the …………… that the earth was at the centre of the universe. **HYPOTHESISE**
The review of the evidence was carried out in an …………… and systematic manner. **ANALYSE**
The police opened a formal …………… into the theft of the laboratory equipment. **INVESTIGATE**
With advances in technology, the movements of storm systems have become far more ……………. **PREDICT**
She …………… chose not to mention the flaw in her argument during the presentation. **DELIBERATE**
His …………… of spoken English was excellent, but his written work needed improvement. **COMPREHEND**
One …………… sign of strong research is the ability to distinguish between correlation and causation. **PERCEIVE**
The essay was marked down for its lack of clear …………… in the central argument. **REASON**

N.title: Negative prefixes with mis-
N.instructions: The prefix mis-, as in misinterpret, is often used to give a positive word a negative meaning. Which of the words in bold in the following sentences use the prefix mis- with this negative sense? Write YES or NO.

questions.sentence: She completely **misunderstood** the professor’s instructions.
The journalist was accused of **misrepresenting** the facts in the article.
The **missile** was tracked across three countries before landing.
He had several **misconceptions** about how human memory actually works.
The singer performed as a **minstrel** at the medieval festival.
Several important details were **misquoted** in the press release.
The **mystery** surrounding the missing data has still not been solved.
She was criticised for **misleading** the public about the study’s findings.
The **mist** over the lake cleared by mid-morning.

O.title: Word formation (Cognitive science text)
O.instructions: Complete the text by writing the correct form of each word in capitals.
O.passage: The …(1)… (HYPOTHESISE) that intelligence is fixed at birth has largely been abandoned by modern cognitive science. This shift in our …(2)… (PERCEIVE) of intellectual capacity has had a significant effect on how we approach education. Rather than seeing a student’s poor performance as simply …(3)… (EXPLAIN), educators are encouraged to take a more …(4)… (INVESTIGATE) approach to identifying the root causes. There is …(5)… (DOUBT) strong evidence that study methods focused on deep …(6)… (COMPREHEND), rather than rote memorisation, produce better long-term results. An …(7)… (ANALYSE) learner is not only able to take in new material but also to evaluate and critique it. Such an approach requires …(8)… (DELIBERATE) and patience from both teacher and student. It also demands …(9)… (REASON) that is clear and well-structured. Ultimately, education should aim to produce graduates who are …(10)… (PREDICT) in neither their interests nor their capabilities – learners who continue to surprise both themselves and others.

questions.sentence: The …(1)… (HYPOTHESISE) that intelligence is fixed at birth has largely been abandoned.
This shift in our …(2)… (PERCEIVE) of intellectual capacity has had a significant effect on how we approach education.
Rather than seeing a student’s poor performance as simply …(3)… (EXPLAIN), educators are encouraged to take a more investigative approach.
Educators are encouraged to take a more …(4)… (INVESTIGATE) approach to identifying the root causes.
There is …(5)… (DOUBT) strong evidence that study methods focused on deep comprehension produce better results.
Study methods focused on deep …(6)… (COMPREHEND), rather than rote memorisation, produce better long-term results.
An …(7)… (ANALYSE) learner is not only able to take in new material but also to evaluate and critique it.
Such an approach requires …(8)… (DELIBERATE) and patience from both teacher and student.
It also demands …(9)… (REASON) that is clear and well-structured.
Education should aim to produce graduates who are …(10)… (PREDICT) in neither their interests nor their capabilities.
